_source: Christopher Anthony / Michael J. Taylor, The Macedonian Phalanx: Equipment, Organization and Tactics from Philip and Alexander to the Roman Conquest (Pen & Sword, 2020)
_provenance: Numbers come from the ancient tacticians (Asclepiodotus c.100 BC, Aelian, Arrian) and Polybius 18.28-32, as reported (and sometimes disputed) by Taylor. prov tags: tacticians = drill-manual figure; polybius = the one contemporary historian; taylor-note = Taylor's caveat/dispute. These are IDEAL/canonical figures; real units varied and intervals were judged by eye, not measured. See ../PROVENANCE.md. cite = line number(s) in the source content.md.
_units: 1 cubit = 1.5 ft = ~0.5 m (Taylor rounds 45cm up to 50cm 'for ease of calculation'); spacing and sarissa lengths given in cubits, with metre conversion.

synaspismos.use: locked shields; static defence / receive a charge
synaspismos.note: Taylor: maybe defensive-only; Polybius never uses it for calculations

early_alexander.note: the phalanx at Issus was probably 8 deep

doubled.note: Seleucids at Magnesia; limits frontage, adds push, resists breakthrough

sarissa.note: Polybius: 16-cubit design reduced to 14 in practice; 4 cubits lost to grip+counterweight => 10 cubits project from the front-rank man; each rank back reaches 2 cubits less => 5 ranks' points project (descending 10/8/6/4/2). Rear-grip model (Asclep./Aelian) loses only 2 cubits.

unit_hierarchy.name: taxis (tactician sense)
unit_hierarchy.note: NB literary 'taxis' = a much larger ~1,500-man unit

unit_hierarchy.name: syntagma / speira / semeia
unit_hierarchy.composition: 16 files x 16 ranks = a square; smallest independent manoeuvre unit

unit_hierarchy.name: diphalangia / keras (wing)
unit_hierarchy.composition: 2 phalangarchiai; the largest subunit (~32 syntagmata, 8,000 ideal)

unit_hierarchy.name: phalanx (tetraphalangarchia)
unit_hierarchy.composition: 2 wings; full idealized doubling (a 'standard' phalanx ~16,000 = 1,000 files)

feasibility_facts: Only the first ~5 ranks (6 in Aelian/Arrian) can reach the enemy with their sarissas; all ranks beyond add NO frontage or reach (Polybius 18.30.1, L13185).
Depth beyond the reaching ranks buys only push/weight, morale/stability, and replacements - not extra spear points; >16 ranks was considered useless for pushing (L13185,L13444).
Depth-vs-frontage trade-off: a deeper line is steadier but narrower and more easily outflanked; a shallower line widens the front (L8827).
Broken/rough/uneven ground opens gaps in the spear-hedge and dislocates the formation - it needs flat, bare ground (Polybius 18.31, L3334; Taylor thinks this is overstated, L3343).
Flanks and rear are the decisive weakness: pikes face only forward, there are no reserves, and once the line is broken or outflanked the phalangite is poorly armed for close fighting (L3632,L3340).
The phalanx fights as a single continuous line; a gap anywhere (terrain, casualties, uneven advance) can be fatal - Romans infiltrated wherever gaps opened (Livy 44.41, L3772).
At 2-cubit close order a Macedonian occupies half a Roman's frontage, so each front-rank Roman faces 2 Macedonians + the points of ~8 ranks behind = ~10 sarissae (Polybius, L10210,L3331).
Synaspismos (1-cubit) is formed by 'doubling by men' (rear half-file steps into the front half's gaps): same men, same frontage, half the interval and half the depth (L10809).

model_constants._note: advise.py geometry: spacing_m = spacing_by_order[order].metres. frontage_m = (men/depth)*spacing_m; depth_m = depth*spacing_m; files = men/depth. men_for_line = (length_m/spacing_m)*depth. projection: rank n projects (sarissa_cubits - grip_loss) - 2*(n-1) cubits beyond the front-rank man; reaching ranks = count of n>=1 with that >0; convert cubits->m via cubit.metres.
